old_claim.note: Central Station Power provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Resistance-heating appliances require electric current as their energy input; Seely's flat iron placed a carbon resistance inside the iron and connected it to a source of electricity.

source_shape.source_locator: Specification, opening paragraphs and claims: the appliance uses electric current to heat flat irons and similar utensils by placing an electrical resistance inside the iron; the patent says current may come from any suitable source.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The patented appliance converts electrical input directly into heat through an internal carbon resistance and does not specify central-station generation as part of the device.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Every realization inside the narrowed resistance-heating scope needs electric current, while a particular generation-and-distribution business model is only one possible way to supply that current.

ontology_before: Early Electrical Appliances depended on Central Station Power, making a specific urban supply architecture appear foundational to a node that also included unrelated motor appliances.
ontology_after: Early Electric Resistance-Heating Appliances depends directly on Electricity as the required energy input; central-station power remains external deployment context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: electricity is the required direct prerequisite and central_station_power is no longer a direct prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped specifically to mass-market appliances sold only for central-station utility service.
A primary source shows Seely's 1882 appliance could operate without electrical current.
A separate early_electric_appliance_distribution node is added whose scope explicitly requires central-station customer service.
short_reason: Electric current is intrinsic; central-station supply is not.
